title: deny.py external-CLI block is bypassed by any multi-line command (no semicolon needed)
description: should_deny_command() (and the regexes it composes: _DENY_AT_CMD_POS, _OMC_TEAM, _SH_C, _EVAL) all anchor 'command position' via `_CMD_POS = r"(?:^|[;&|(`]|\|\||&&)"`. None of the regexes use re.MULTILINE, and the class does not include a bare newline. `^` therefore only matches the very start of the whole string. Any run_terminal_command/Bash payload that puts the denied binary (claude/codex/omx/agy/cursor-agent/kimi) on a line by itself, preceded only by a newline (not `;`, `&`, `|`, backtick, `&&`/`||`), is invisible to the matcher. Multi-line shell scripts are the normal way agents pass Bash tool commands (heredocs, sequential setup + run steps), so this is not an edge case — it is the common shape of real tool calls.
failure_scenario: A Bash/run_terminal_command call with command string `"echo start\nclaude -p 'do something'"` (two lines, no semicolon) is sent through pre_tool_use_deny.py. Verified directly: `should_deny_command('echo start\nclaude -p "hello"')` returns False, while the semicolon-joined equivalent `'echo start; claude -p hi'` correctly returns True. decide_pre_tool_use() therefore returns `{"decision": "allow"}` and the PreToolUse hook lets the run_terminal_command execute claude (or codex/agy/kimi/cursor-agent, or `omc team`) — exactly the class of invocation the module exists to block, and one that requires no special evasion technique, just an ordinary multi-line script.

title: verdict.py run_id binding can be bypassed by an unrelated JSON blob with no run_id key
description: _json_verdict() only enforces the expected_run_id check when the candidate JSON object itself contains a 'run_id' key (`if expected_run_id is not None and (strict or "run_id" in data):`). parse_verdict()/_extract_json_objects() scan the whole artifact text for ALL fenced ```json blocks plus the first bare balanced-brace object, not just one canonical document. Any second JSON object found in the text that omits schema_version/run_id (e.g. an example snippet, a stray tool-call log fragment, or a copy of the desired schema the reviewer pasted while explaining) is evaluated with the run_id check fully skipped, so `{"verdict": "APPROVE"}` alone is enough to make parse_verdict() return APPROVE — completely defeating the anti-stale/anti-replay run_id binding the module's own docstring describes as the 'Codex P0 / 2026-07-20 council' fix. This function backs the actual verifier gate: dual_review.py:401 calls `parse_verdict_file(verifier_art, expected_run_id=run_id)` and ralplan.py:300-302 call `artifact_contains_approve(..., expected_run_id=run_id)`.
failure_scenario: Verified directly with the real module: text containing `{"run_id": "WRONG-STALE-RUN", "verdict": "FAILED"}` (the real, correctly-bound verdict, which is a mismatch/FAILED) plus, elsewhere in the same document, a fenced ```json block `{"verdict": "APPROVE"}` with no run_id field — `parse_verdict(text, expected_run_id='REAL-RUN-123')` returns `'APPROVE'`. Any verifier/critic transcript that happens to contain an unguarded example or leftover JSON snippet saying APPROVE (a very plausible LLM-authored artifact, e.g. 'the correct format looks like: ```json\n{"verdict": "APPROVE"}\n```' while its real verdict for this run is REQUEST_CHANGES/FAILED/stale) causes dual_review/ralplan's strict-v2 gate to accept a run that was never actually approved for this run_id.

title: command_policy.py --no-allowlist break-glass still permits python code execution via bundled short flags (e.g. -ic)
description: The module docstring and check_command_policy() promise `python* -c / -e` is a 'hard floor' that is 'never liftable via --allow-cmd / break-glass'. Under normal (non-break-glass) operation this holds because _check_python_argv() rejects any argv[i] flag it doesn't explicitly recognize. But under `no_allowlist=True` (the TTY-only --no-allowlist break-glass path used by `omg accept --no-allowlist`), check_command_policy() runs only the floor check `_has_flag(cmd, "-c", "-e")` and then returns immediately (lines 666-674) — _check_python_argv() is never called. _has_flag()'s glued-short-option detection only matches tokens that literally start with '-c'/'-e' (e.g. '-cCODE'); it does not detect combined POSIX short options like '-ic' where the real CPython interpreter treats it as '-i' + '-c'. Verified: `python3 -ic "print(1)"` executes as `python3 -i -c "print(1)"` on the real interpreter.
failure_scenario: Verified against the real module: `check_command_policy(['python3','-ic','print(1)'])` (no break-glass) is correctly denied ('python flag -ic not allowed'), but `check_command_policy(['python3','-ic','import os,sys; os.system("id")'], no_allowlist=True)` returns without raising — i.e. is ALLOWED. Any PRD story or `omg accept --run <id> --no-allowlist` acceptance command using `python3 -ic '<arbitrary code>'` runs arbitrary code during acceptance despite the operator being told 'Shells, agent CLIs, python -c, npx, and always-deny bins still blocked' for break-glass mode.

title: Autopilot FSM: blocked→implement→blocked round-trip lets new code reach acceptance/verified without a fresh review or QA pass
description: LEGAL_TRANSITIONS (lines 26-38) allows both `qa` and `implement` to transition directly to `blocked`, and `blocked` to transition directly to `implement` or `qa`. The staleness-invalidation logic in `transition()` (lines 273-292) only calls `invalidate_quality_stages()` when entering `ralplan` from {review, qa}, when entering `rework`, or when entering `review` from {rework, implement}. There is no invalidation branch for entering `implement` (from any source, including `blocked`). Meanwhile `stage_review_is_clean()`/`stage_qa_is_clean()` (lines 59-88) only check `writer`, `run_id`, and `invalidated`/`clean` flags on the on-disk stamp — they never compare against the current git diff/product hash (confirmed in omg_cli/review.py: the `diff_hash` binding is only checked once, at the moment `run_structured_review()` writes the stamp; it is never re-validated at gate-check time). So once a review/QA stamp is clean=True, it stays authoritative for `stage_review_is_clean`/`stage_qa_is_clean` until an explicit invalidating transition occurs — and `blocked→implement` is not one of them. The `next_phase == "implement"` gate (lines 253-255) only requires a trivially-suppliable `evidence={"consensus": true}` JSON blob passed by the CLI caller via `--evidence-json`, with no cryptographic or artifact-backed verification.
failure_scenario: 1) `omg autopilot transition --phase implement --evidence-json '{"consensus":true}'`, then real review/QA agents produce clean stamps; `transition review→qa` and the real QA cycle both succeed (stage_review_is_clean/stage_qa_is_clean both True). 2) Operator/agent runs `omg autopilot transition --run <id> --phase blocked --reason infra-hiccup` from `qa` (legal edge `qa→blocked`); no stamps are invalidated. 3) `omg autopilot transition --run <id> --phase implement --evidence-json '{"consensus":true}'` (legal edge `blocked→implement`); real, unreviewed product-code changes are made in this phase. 4) `omg autopilot transition --run <id> --phase blocked --reason another-hiccup` (legal edge `implement→blocked`); still no invalidation. 5) `omg autopilot transition --run <id> --phase qa` (legal edge `blocked→qa`) succeeds because `stage_review_is_clean()` still reads the stale clean=True stamp from step 1, unrelated to the new diff from step 3. 6) `omg autopilot transition --run <id> --phase acceptance` succeeds because `stage_qa_is_clean()` is likewise stale-clean. 7) `omg autopilot complete` runs `freeze_and_run` (acceptance tests only, not code review/QA scenarios) and, if the acceptance suite doesn't happen to cover the new diff, sets `verified=True`. The step-3 code changes were never seen by structured_review.json or ultraqa.json, defeating the FSM's core guarantee that verified code passed review+QA.
suggested_fix: Invalidate review/QA stamps (call invalidate_quality_stages) on every transition INTO `implement`, regardless of source phase (not just when entering `review` from rework/implement). Additionally, bind stage_review_is_clean/stage_qa_is_clean to a live product/diff hash captured at gate-check time (e.g. compare qa.py's product_hash(root) or review.py's diff_hash against the current git HEAD) instead of trusting a disk flag that can outlive unrelated intervening code changes.

title: Ownership join silently skips file-ownership enforcement when a manifest task has empty owned_files
description: `join_worker_results()` computes `foreign = sorted(changed_norm - owned) if owned else []` (line 531). If a task's `owned_files` set is empty, `foreign` is unconditionally `[]`, so the `ownership_violation` check (line 532) can never trigger for that task no matter what files its envelope claims to have changed. `build_ownership_manifest()` normally rejects empty `owned_files` (raises WorkerError), so this path isn't reachable through the intended API, but `load_ownership_manifest()` (lines 442-452) only validates `writer == CLI_WRITER` on the loaded JSON and does not re-validate that every task still has non-empty `owned_files` — so a manually edited or otherwise malformed on-disk manifest with an empty `owned_files` array for a task silently disables the cross-task file-ownership guard for that task.
failure_scenario: A `task_ownership.json` on disk (e.g. hand-edited during debugging, or produced by a future code path that doesn't go through build_ownership_manifest's validation) contains a task entry with `"owned_files": []`. That task's worker seals an envelope claiming `status: ok` with `changed_files` covering another task's owned file. `join_worker_results` computes `foreign = []` (since `owned` is falsy) and reports `status: ok` with no `ownership_violation`, so `integrate_results` proceeds to cherry-pick that worker's commits even though it wrote outside its assigned slice — exactly the cross-task collision the ownership manifest exists to prevent.
suggested_fix: In join_worker_results, treat an empty owned_files set as fail-closed: if `owned` is empty, flag ownership_violation for any non-empty `changed_files` (or reject the manifest task at load time in load_ownership_manifest, mirroring build_ownership_manifest's own non-empty requirement).

title: docs/skills.md documents non-existent `omg goal start`/`omg goal complete` subcommands (real CLI is start-story/complete-story)
description: docs/skills.md's `omg-ultragoal` reference table states: `| **CLI** | omg goal init|status|start|checkpoint|complete|link-run|verify|repair |`. The actual argparse subparsers registered in omg_cli/main.py (goal_sub.add_parser(...)) are `init, status, link-run, start-story, checkpoint, block-story, resume-story, complete-story, verify, repair` — there is no `start` or `complete` action, and `block-story`/`resume-story` are omitted from the doc entirely. The correct usage is documented correctly elsewhere (skills/omg-ultragoal/SKILL.md uses start-story/complete-story throughout), so this is an isolated drift in the human-facing catalog doc. The identical bug is duplicated in docs/skills.zh-Hant.md line 210. scripts/check_docs_links.py (the only docs CI guard) never cross-checks omg subcommand names against omg_cli/main.py, so this drift is invisible to CI.
failure_scenario: A user reads docs/skills.md, copies `omg goal start --goal G --story s1`, and runs it. Verified live: `./bin/omg goal start --help` → `omg goal: error: argument goal_action: invalid choice: 'start' (choose from init, status, link-run, start-story, checkpoint, block-story, resume-story, complete-story, verify, repair)`. Same failure for `omg goal complete`. The user has to reverse-engineer the real subcommand names from `--help` or the SKILL.md instead of trusting the catalog doc.
suggested_fix: Replace `init|status|start|checkpoint|complete|link-run|verify|repair` with `init|status|link-run|start-story|checkpoint|block-story|resume-story|complete-story|verify|repair` in docs/skills.md and docs/skills.zh-Hant.md, and add an automated check (e.g. extend scripts/check_docs_links.py or a new test) that diffs every `omg <cmd> <subaction>` string appearing in docs/skills.md against the actual argparse choices in omg_cli/main.py.

title: install-plugin.sh's documented "upgrade" flow does not refresh the installed plugin snapshot; `omg doctor` never detects the staleness
description: `grok plugin install` copies a local-path plugin into a frozen snapshot under `~/.grok/installed-plugins/<repo_key>/` (confirmed: it is a real git checkout, not a symlink). install-plugin.sh's own comment on line 21-22 (`"already installed" is OK — still refresh global hooks below`) shows the author already knows `grok plugin install <path> --trust` on an already-installed local source can no-op/return non-zero, yet the script never calls the host's actual refresh command, `grok plugin update <name>` (confirmed to exist via `grok plugin update --help`). README.md's 'Upgrade' row tells users the fix is simply `git pull && ./scripts/install-plugin.sh`, which is not sufficient. This was reproduced live in this repo: the installed snapshot at `~/.grok/installed-plugins/-ed6f3e28` (and a second copy at `oh-my-grok-7faf0130`) is pinned at git commit 7958cab (the v0.3.1 release tag), while the working checkout is 3+ commits and one release ahead at 46b3489 / plugin.json version 0.3.2. `diff` of skills/omg-autopilot/SKILL.md between the installed copy and the repo shows real content divergence (the v0.3.2 pytest-marker-quoting / auto-PRD guidance is present in the repo but absent from what the live Grok session actually loads). Worse, `omg doctor` — the exact tool the README and this skill tell users to run to confirm install health — prints `plugin.json: valid (oh-my-grok@0.3.2)` on one line and `plugin trust/inventory: ... version=0.3.1 ...` a few lines later, but omg_cli/doctor.py's `_summarize_plugin_payload()` never compares the two version strings; both lines print `[OK]` and the whole run ends `all hard checks passed`.
failure_scenario: Maintainer ships a v0.3.2 bugfix to a SKILL.md (e.g. the QA-freeze quoting fix), bumps plugin.json, tags v0.3.2, tells users to `git pull && ./scripts/install-plugin.sh`. A user who already had the plugin installed at v0.3.1 runs the upgrade command exactly as documented; `grok plugin install . --trust` reports 'already installed' (swallowed by the script's `||` fallback) and the on-disk snapshot Grok actually loads stays at v0.3.1. `omg doctor` reports all-OK. The user's live session keeps following the old, buggy SKILL.md instructions with zero warning, believing they are on the latest version because `omg doctor` and `plugin.json` both claim 0.3.2.
suggested_fix: In install-plugin.sh, after the install call, explicitly run `grok plugin update oh-my-grok` (or detect 'already installed' and fall back to it) so the local-path snapshot is force-refreshed on every re-run. In omg_cli/doctor.py's plugin-inventory check, compare the installed `version` field against the local plugin.json version and emit a hard `[FAIL]`/`[WARN]` (not `[OK]`) when they diverge.

title: `grok plugin install` creates duplicate same-named plugin entries when the source path differs (e.g. "." vs absolute path); relocate/upgrade flow never reconciles or removes the stale one
description: `grok plugin install <source> --trust` keys installations by a hash derived from the literal source string, not the resolved real path. `grok plugin list --json` in this repo shows two separate 'installed' entries both named 'oh-my-grok', one with `source: '.../oh-my-grok/.'` (repo_key `-ed6f3e28`) and one with `source: '.../oh-my-grok'` (repo_key `oh-my-grok-7faf0130`) — the same directory, two independent plugin registrations, both stale at v0.3.1 (see prior finding). README.md's 'Relocate clone' row instructs users to just re-run `./scripts/install-plugin.sh` at the new location; it never tells them to first `grok plugin uninstall oh-my-grok` at the old path. Since both entries share the identical plugin name, `grok plugin uninstall oh-my-grok` (the exact command README's 'Uninstall plugin' row gives) is ambiguous about which physical install it targets, and `omg_cli/doctor.py`'s `_summarize_plugin_payload()` (omg_cli/doctor.py L320-325) picks whichever same-named entry appears *first* in the `grok plugin list --json` array — not necessarily the one that corresponds to the current working directory's checkout — so `omg doctor`'s 'plugin trust/inventory' line can silently report facts about a different, possibly orphaned, install path than the one the user is actually working in.
failure_scenario: A user moves their clone from `~/.local/share/oh-my-grok` to `~/dev/oh-my-grok` per the README's relocate instructions and re-runs install-plugin.sh. This creates a *second* `oh-my-grok`-named plugin registration rather than replacing the old one; the old, now-orphaned path's plugin (pointing at a directory that may no longer exist or is stale) can still be the one `grok inspect`/`omg doctor` picks up first, and `grok plugin uninstall oh-my-grok` cannot cleanly target 'the new one' vs 'the old one' by name alone.
suggested_fix: Before installing, install-plugin.sh should check `grok plugin list --json` for any existing entry named `oh-my-grok` whose `source` path differs from the current `$ROOT`, and either `grok plugin uninstall oh-my-grok` it first or warn the user loudly with the stale repo_key/path so they can clean it up manually. `omg doctor`'s plugin-inventory probe should also filter candidates by matching `source`/`path` against the actual project root instead of taking the first name match.

title: README's plugin-only install path claims `omg` commands work but omits that .omg/ scaffolding (`omg setup`) requires the omg CLI, which plugin-only install explicitly does not provide
description: The 'Plugin-only (half surface)' section correctly states this path 'does not put `omg` on PATH', but the very next section ('Recommended default flow', lines 124-153) and the in-session skill tables immediately tell the reader to run `omg interview start`, `omg ralplan`, `omg accept --yes`, etc., with no explicit reminder at the point of use that these all require the Full install. A reader who followed only the Plugin-only path and then follows the Quick start's 'Smoke after install' (`omg doctor`) will hit `command not found: omg` with no link back to the caveat they already scrolled past.
failure_scenario: User runs only `grok plugin install ImL1s/oh-my-grok --trust` (plugin-only path), then in the same README continues to 'Smoke after install' and types `omg doctor` — `zsh: command not found: omg` — with no inline pointer back to the 'Full install' section to fix it.
suggested_fix: Add a one-line callout directly above 'Smoke after install' / 'Recommended default flow': 'Requires Full install (omg on PATH) — plugin-only skips this.'
